text: ARNEMUYDEN, a seaport town of Holland, 6 m. NE of Flushing, in the island of Walcheren, supposed to be the ancient Hanse town, Arnemunda. Pop. 1,200. It has a considerable trade in salt. It formerly had a very good harbour; but it is now nearly filled with sand and mud. The remains of strong fortifications exist here.
ARNESBY, a parish in Guthlaxton hund., Leicestershire. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 505.
ARNESEIO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Bari, 7 m. SW of Andria, in N lat. 41° 10'.
ARNEVAL, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, S of Saarbruck.
ARNFELS, a village of Styria, in the circle of Marburg, 10 m. SE of Landsberg. The neighbourhood is celebrated for its red wine. Pop. 350.
ARNGASK, a parish of Scotland, comprehending a part of three counties: viz., Fifeshire on the E, Perthshire on the W and NW, and Kinross-shire on the S. Pop. in 1821, 680; in 1841, 750.
ARNGITZES, a town of Wallachia, 44 m. SE of Hermannstadt.
ARNHAUSEN, a town of Bayaria, in the principality of Wurtzburg, 2 m. S of Kissingen.—Also a town of Prussia, in Pomerania, 24 m. NW of New Stettin.

ARNHEIM, or ARNEHEIM, a town of Gueldreland, in Holland, on the r. bank of the Old Rhine, 30 m. E of Utrecht, in N lat. 51° 58′ 47″, E long. 5° 54½. Pop. 15,000. It belonged to the confederacy of Hanse towns, and was originally the residence of the dukes of Gueldreland, and afterwards of the provincial prefect. It is well-situated for trade, is neatly built, and adorned with several fine ecclesiastical edifices, particularly the churches of St. Eusebius and St. Walburg. It has extensive fortifications, the work of the celebrated Cohorn, which are now planted with elms, and afford an agreeable promenade. A market for corn, cattle, and fish, is held here. The trade is chiefly transit trade along the river, and between Amsterdam and Germany by land.

ARNHEIM BAY, an inlet at the NW extremity of the gulf of Carpentaria, in Australia, in S lat. 12° 11', E long. 136° 3', at the entrance. It has good anchorage ground, and about 100 sq. m. of roadsted.
ARNHEIM'S LAND, a district of Australia, on the W side of Carpentaria bay. It is well-wooded, and rises gently from the shore to a moderate elevation.
ARNHEIM (CAPE), a smooth grassy promontory on the coast of the above district, in S lat. 12° 19', E long. 137° 1'.

ARNO, anciently Arnus, one of the largest and finest rivers of Italy, which divides Tuscany into two parts, and washes the cities Florence and Pisa. It rises in the Apennines, on the southern slope of Monte Falterona, at an alt. of 4,430 ft., near a village called Santa Maria della Grazia, 20 m. W of the sources of the Tiber, in N lat. 43° 52'. Descending by the village of Stia, where it has an alt. of 1,575 ft., into the valley of Casentino, and having passed Bibbiena, it turns southwards towards Arezzo, where it is increased by the lakes of the Val di Chiana, a low flat valley so nearly level that a small mound run across it would suffice to cause its waters to drain off either northwards into the Arno or southwards into the Tiber. It then passes through the Laterina, and the deep gorge of the Valle dell' Inferno, into the celebrated Val d'Arno, through which it flows in a NNW direction. Passing Rignano, it receives the Siene, and then sweeping to the W, enters the plain of Florence, flows through that city, and 10 m. below San Miniato, enters the plain of Pisa, whose beautiful buildings are reflected in its tranquil bosom, and falls 4 m. below it, into the Tuscan sea, in N lat. 43° 41', E long. 10° 15′, after a course of about 150 geograph. m. It is navigable by boats from Florence to the Mediterranean, a distance of 60 m. At the quay of Florence it is 160 ft. above sea-level; and is precisely the breadth of the Seine before the Tuileries; but is artificially enlarged by means of dams below the city. [Simond.] This river has been celebrated by ancient and by modern poets, on account of the beautiful banks between which it flows, and the rich scenes with which they are adorned. From any hill in the neighbourhood of Florence, or at the confluence of the Chiana, the view into the extensive valley of the A., "decorated with innumerable buildings and distant towns rising among the richest possible cultivation of the olive and the vine" [Williams], is charming; yet, in a picturesque point of view, the lower part of the Val d'Arno is little remarkable. It is, however, highly cultivated; and is perhaps the spot of all Italy where the land is most subdivided, and the pop. most numerous. In very ancient times, the Etruscans erected extensive works of hydraulic architecture on the banks of the A., long before any other Italian nation had arrived at such a degree of civilization. Niebuhr says:-"The rich valley, through which the A. flows, was, in ancient times, covered by a lake and marshes. From Segna to Fiesole, and toward Prato, was one lake; the Gonfalina closed up the valley; a passage was made through this rock, to open a way for the river towards Pisa. The water covered this space at the time of the erection of the walls of Fiesole, as is shown by many openings which were designed for draining it off. It covered the site of modern Florence also, whose origin it is therefore absurd to refer to the Etruscan times. A section was cut at L'Ancisa to drain the rich fields of the upper valley of the A.; or it may be that the rivers, which now form this part of the A., formerly fell into the Clanis, and that the object was to diminish the water of the Tiber. The marshes through which Hannibal marched are at present dry on the r. bank of the Lower A."

ARNO, a river in Lower Calabria, rising in Mount Tupa, and uniting with the Neto.—Also an island in the North Pacific ocean, in N lat. 8° 0', E long, 173° 0'.-Also an island in the Baltic, off the coast of Sweden, in N lat. 58° 8', E long. 16° 45'.
ARNO (CIVITA D'), a town of Italy, in the Statesof-the-Church, 3 m. NE of Perugiano.

ARNOLD, a village and parish in the co. of Nottingham, 4 m. from Nottingham. Area of p. 4,670 acres. Pop. 4,509.—Also a township in the N division of Holderness, in the E. R. of Yorkshire. Pop. 154.—Also a river of Lower Canada, which rises in the township of Clinton and enters Lake Megantick. It received its name from General Arnold, who, in 1775, brought a detachment down this river, on his way to the siege of Quebec.

ARNOLDSDORF, a village of Prussia in Silesia, ARN circle of Schmeidnitz. Pop. 500.—Also a village of Silesia, in the circle of Neisse, near Bischofskuppe, one of the highest mountains in the Riesengebirge range. Pop. 1,200.
ARNOLDSTEIN, a town of Austria, in Carinthia, in the gov. of Laibach, on the Gail, 7 m. SW of Villach.

ARNON, a small river of France, in the dep. of Cher, which joins the Cher below Vierzon, after a course of 87 m. from S to N, in which it passes Cullan and Charrot.—Also a river of Syria, which rises in the mountains of Gilead, and flows through a deep rugged channel called the Wadi Nuzeb, into the Dead sea. Its course is about 60 m.
ARNOSORA, a town of Naples, 12 m. SE of Salerno, in Principato Citra.
ARNOTA, a town of Turkey, in Wallachia, 18 m. W of Kimnick.
ARNOUL, a small river of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inférieure. It has a course of about 20 m. from SSE to NNW, and joins the canal of Brouage. It is sometimes called the canal of Pont l'Abbé.

ARNOULT (Sr.), a commune and town of France, dep. of Seine-Inférieure, cant. of Caudebec. It contains several cotton manufactories. Pop. 946.—Also a commune and village in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Formerie. There is here a manufactory of spinning machines.
ARNOULT-EN-IVELINE (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Dourdan, 6 m. SE of Rambouillet. Pop. 1,420. It is a place of considerable manufacturing industry.
ARNOYA, a river of Spain, which joins the Minho near Ribadavia, in Galicia.—Also a river of Portugal, which falls into the Atlantic, in N lat. 39° 25'.

ARNSBERG, an administrative division of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, bounded on the NW by Munster; NE by Minden; E by Waldeck and Hesse-Darmstadt; on the SE and S by Nassau; on the SW by the district of Coblentz; and on the W by Cologne and Dusseldorf. Area 2,950 sq. m. Pop. 462,052 in 1831; of whom 252,465 were Protestants, 205,044 Catholics, and 4,484 Jews. It is divided into the circles of Arnsberg, Altena, Brilon, Bochum, Dortmund, Hagen, Hamm, Iserlohn, Lippstadt, Meschede, Olpe, Siegen, Sost, and Wittgenstein. This territory was transferred to Prussia from Hesse-Darmstadt in 1813. Its cap., of the same name, is situated on the Ruhr, 40 m. SSE of Munster, at an alt. of 634 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 51° 22'. Pop. in 1837, 4,006. There is a Catholic gymnasium here, and a hofgericht or civil court of appeal.

ARNSDORF, a small town of Lower Bavaria, district of Pfarrkirchen, on the Colbach. Pop. 500. Here are several linen and woollen manufactories. -Also a village of Silesia, in the circle of Hirschberg, with a large paper manufactory. Pop. 1,500.
ARNSHAUGK, a village of Saxe-Weimar, near Neustadt. Pop. 139.

ARNSTADT, a town of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, on the Gera, at an alt. of 900 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 5,000. It is a well-built town, and has a considerable trade in corn, wool, wood, skins, &c. In the vicinity are several copper-mines, said to be the richest in Saxony.

ARNSTEIN, a town in the duchy of Brandenburg, 20 m. S of Brandenburg.—Also a town of Bavaria, 20 m. NE of Wurtzburg, on the Wehm. Its trade consists in wine and grain. Pop. 1,396. It was the birth-place of Ignat. Schmidt the historian.
ARNSTORF, a market-town of Austria, in the duchy of Carinthia, 6 m. W of Mauttern.
ARNSWALDE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, 21 m. NW of Friedberg, on a lake formed by the Ihna. Pop. of town and circle in 1837, 3,986.
ARNUAL, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, on the l. bank of the Saar. Pop. 874.
ARO, a river of Spain, in Catalonia, which enters the sea 6 m. S of Palamos, in N lat. 41° 50'.

AROA, a town of Venezuela, in the prov. of Carabobo, in N lat. 10° 35', on a river of the same name, which takes its rise in the mountains of San Felippe, and enters the Golfo Triste in the Caribbean sea. It is the channel of communication with the Bolivar copper-mines, situate about 60 m. from the coast, whence, in 1835, nearly 5,000 tons of copper were exported to England.

AROAN, a station in the desert of Sahara, in Western Africa, on the route from Marocco to Timbuctu, in N lat. 18° 51'. Salt mines are worked here, and the produce is transported to Nigritia. Pop. about 3,000.
AROANHA, a river of South Africa, which falls into the channel of Mozambique, in S lat. 16° 45'.
AROCHE, a town of Spain, in Seville, prov. of Huelva, in N lat. 37° 52', near the Aroche mountains, which run from SW to NE, and join the Sierra Morena.

AROHA, a chain of basaltic hills on the coast of the northern island of New Zealand, running southward from Cape Colville to about the parallel of 38° S, forming in the first 90 m. of its course the eastern boundary of the frith of Thames, and, as it stretches to the S, the eastern boundary of the basin of the Thames river. It consists of trap rocks, having a mean alt., according to Dieffenbach, of 1,500 ft., but according to Jamieson of 4,000 ft., and covered from their crests downwards with gloomy and stately forests.
AROK-SZALAS, a town in Hungary, 11 m. N of Jasz-Bereny, on the Gyöngyös. Pop. 7,706. It has a large trade in corn, and is the principal mercantile entrepot between Pest and Upper Hungary.
AROLSEN, a town of Germany, in the principality of Waldeck-Pyrmont, on the river Ahr, in N lat. 51° 23'. Pop. 2,050. It is the residence of the prince and his court.

ARON, a river of France, in the dep. of Nievre, rising in a lake of the same name, a little to the S of Saint-Reverien, and entering the Loire after a course of 51 m., in which it passes Chatillon-en-Bazois, Anizy, Ysenai, and Cercy. The navigation, as well as the floatage of this river, are greatly impeded by the narrowness of the stream at Deux-Pertuis; but during four months of the year, timber can be floated down, and it is navigable for 18 m. from Ysenai to the Loire.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, 2 m. E of Mayenne. Pop. 1,530.—Also a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak-Adjemi, 3 m. NW of Kashan. It contains 2,000 houses, and a manufacture of silk-goods is carried on in it.

ARONA, a fortified town of Sardinia, on the r. bank and near the Sextremity of the Lago Maggiore, 12 m. S of Palenza, in N lat. 45° 46'. Pop. 5,000. It is handsomely built, and contains four churches, an hospital, a gymnasium, and a ruinous palace in which Carolo Borroméo was born. It has a harbour on the lake, and carries on a considerable trade, as all the merchandise sent from Genoa and the Sardinian states to Switzerland, passes by this place. On a neighbouring eminence there is a colossal statue of Borroméo, 66 ft. in height, on a granite pedestal of 46 ft. The head, hands, and feet are of bronze; the drapery is composed of sheets of copper on timber-framing. It was erected in 1697, and is considered the chefd'œuvre of Zanelli and Falconi. There are quarries of red marble in the vicinity.

AROOSTOOK, a river in the state of Maine, U. S., which joins the St. John's river on the W borders of New Brunswick, in N lat. 46° 48', a little above the ARQ mouth of the Tobique, which joins the St. John on the opposite or l. bank. It rises nearly under the meridian of 69° W. It is navigable by boats for 40 m.
AROPILLY, a town of Hindostan in Mysore, 20 m. S of Bangalore.
AROS, a village and harbour in the island of Mull, in Scotland, with the ruins of a castle of the Macdonald family, 18 m. NW of Achnaeraig.

AROSA, a bay on the W coast of Galicia, in Spain, between Cape Salvora and Point St. Vincent. Its shores are generally shoal and rocky. It contains an island of the same name; and the isle Salvora, about 1 m. in length, and high in the middle, stretches across its mouth.
AROUAS, a river in French Guayana. It falls into the Maroni, 156 m. WSW of Cayenne, after a course from S to N of about 78 m.
AROUCA, a town of Beira, in Portugal, 27 m. SW of Lamego. Pop. 5,500.
AROUI, a river in Spanish Guayana, which falls into the Orinoco, 60 m. W from St. Thomas, after a course of nearly 150 m. from S to N.
ARP, a river of Circassia, which joins the Kuban in N lat. 44° 46'.
ARPAIA, a town of Naples, in Principato Ultra, 6 m. SW of Benevento.

ARPAJON, anciently Châtres, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, arrond. of Corbeil. The cant. comprises 19 com. The commune and town, with a pop. of 2,165, are 21 m. S of Versailles, in a fertile valley near the river Orge, upon the road from Paris to Orleans. Here are several manufactories, and the town has a brisk trade in corn and flour.
ARPAJON, or Severac, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal, 2 m. SE of Aurillac. Pop. 2,234.

ARPASU, or Arpashai, —the Harpasus of Xenophon—a river of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Kars, in Turkish Armenia, rising near Lake Madetepeh, and flowing into the Aras or Araxes at a point 51 m. W of Erivan.—Also a river of Russian Armenia, in the khanate of Erivan, which rises SW of the Kaplan-Dagh, near the deep valley of Daralaguz, and falls into the Aras at the village of Shirur, 40 40 m. SE of the mouth of the Gurni. It forms the frontier between the districts of Erivan and Nakchivan, and is denominated the Eastern Arpasu, or Arpashai, to distinguish it from the former Arpasu, which constitutes the western frontier of the district.

ARPINO, a small town of the Terra di Lavora, in Naples, 7 m. S of Sora. Pop. 11,060. It occupies the site of the ancient Arpinum. It has some manufactures of woollen cloth and paper, and is the birth-place of Marius and Cicero.

ARQUA, a village of Lombardy, in the prov. and 10 m. S of Padua. Pop. 1,200. It is remarkable for the house and the tomb of Petrarch, who died at his villa here in 1374. The poet is laid—for he cannot be said to be buried-in a sarcophagus of red marble, raised, on four pilasters, on an elevated base, and overshadowed by four laurels.-Also a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, prov. of Polisini, 5 m. SW of Rovigo. Its trade consists in silk, cattle, and agricultural produce. Pop. 3,000.—Also a well-built town in Mexico, between Lagos and Zacatecas. Pop. 4,000.
ARQUATTA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Genoa, 25 m. N of that city.—Also a town in the States-of-the-Church, 31 m. W of Spoleto, on the Tronto.
ARQUENNES, a village of the Netherlands, in the prov. of Hainault, 18 m. NW of Charleroi. Pop. 1,667. Marble and limestone are wrought here.

ARQUES, a village of France, in the dep. of Pas- * de-Calais, 1 m. from Saint-Omer. Pop. 2,190.—Also a commune and village in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Cayals. Pop. 724.—Also a town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, on the river Arques, 4 m. SE of Dieppe. Pop. 934. The battle of Arques was fought in this neighbourhood in 1589, when Henry IV. defeated the Duke du Mayenne.-The river A. has a course of about 20 m., and is navigable to the town of A. Its tributaries are the Bethune and the Eaulne.
ARRA, a town in the pash. of Diyarbekir in Asiatic Turkey, 30 m. W of Orfa.

ARRABIDA, a fort of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 5 m. SW of Setubal. It is on the N bank of the Saado, and is built on the summit of a mountain of the same name, rising 1,000 ft. above the level of the sea, and presenting very wild and romantic scenery.
ARRACAN. See ARACAN.

ARRACOVA, a village of Greece, 9 m. higher up Parnassus than Delphi. When Clarke visited it, it contained 250 houses, inhabited by Albanians and Greeks. It is celebrated for the excellence of its vines, which are cultivated with great labour on terraces formed on the side of the mountain. From this v. to the summit is about 4 hours of ascent.
ARRADON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, 3 m. SW of Vannes. Pop. 1,364.
ARRAGON. See ARAGON.

ARRAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, in N lat. 25° 32'. It is a place of considerable size, and is well-peopled.—Also a town in the prov. of Mekran, in Persia, on the coast of the Arabian sea, 140 m. W of Tatta, in N lat. 25° 25'.
ARRAHMERA (CAPE), or St. Anthony, a promontory on the southern coast of Arabia, in N lat. 12° 40′, E long. 44° 10′.
ARRAI, a town on the coast of the island of Nifon, 110 m. SW of Jeddo. Pop. 800.
ARRAMECHA, a town of Malwah, in Hindostan, 4 m. SE from Kairwass. Pop. 300.

ARRAN, a mountainous island of Scotland, in the frith of Clyde, between Kintyre in Argyleshire, and Cunningham in Ayrshire, forming part of the shire of Bute. It is nearly 20 m. in length from NNW to SSE, and about 11 m. in greatest breadth. Its area amounts to 105,814 acres; of which 14,431 are under cultivation. It contained, in 1831, 6,427, and in 1841, 6,241 inhabitants, who chiefly reside in small villages on the coasts, which are indented with several safe creeks. The mountainous parts of the island are either bare rocks, or covered with heath and fern; the lower grounds are poor, and but indifferently cultivated. The chief produce is oats, bear or big, potatoes, pease, and beans. The surface is now intersected and skirted with excellent roads; and the whole of the uncultivated land is enclosed and divided into sheep-farms. The mountains are chiefly collected on the northern half of the island; from Goatfell southwards, they rapidly diminish into moderate hills of recent formation. Trap occurs in the southern part of the island. A. is "the very jewel of geologists," and since the days of Hutton has attracted the attention of all the leading disciples of that science in Europe. Playfair, Jamieson, Neckar, Macculloch, Sedgewick, Murchison, and Ramsay have made it the scene of their explorations, and have contributed to elucidate its geology. The inhabitants are strongly attached to their native soil, and are partial to a maritime life. Arran contains two parishes: Kilbride and Kilmory, with a sort of chapel-of-ease attached to each. Brodick, the principal village, is finely situated on a small bay at the foot of Goatfell,-a mountain which rises to the ARR height of 2,863 ft. above sea-level. The other villages are Lamlash and Currie. Lamlash-bay, sheltered by Holy island, affords safe anchorage for any number of vessels. The language chiefly spoken is Gaelic, though the people generally understand English. Its distance from the nearest point of the island of Bute is about 5 m.; and from the nearest point in Kintyre, 4 m. The islet of Pladda, off the SE point of Arran-in N lat. 55° 25′ 34", W long. 5° 7′ 9"—has a lighthouse upon it. The cairns, monumental stones, and other relics of Druidism which still exist here, seem to indicate that this island must have been of considerable note in ancient times. The inhabitants have traditions of the hero Fingal, and of Ossian, who is said to have died here. The greater part of the island has been for several centuries in possession of the family of Hamilton.

ARRAN ISLES, or South Arran Islands, three islands called Arranmore, Ennismain, and Innishere, near the W coast of Ireland, at the mouth of Galway bay, extending 12 m. ESE and WNW. They contain about 11,288 acres, and yield a rental of about £2,000. The population, in 1821, was 3,079; in 1841, 3,520. The inhabitants are mostly engaged in fishing, for which purpose they use a boat made of wicker-work, and covered with tarred cloth. The largest village in these islands is Killany, in Arranmore, where a pier and landing-quay have been erected.
ARRANCY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. and 6 m. N of Spincourt.

ARRANMORE (NORTH), an island in co. Donegal, 13 m. W of Arteen point. Length 3 m.; breadth 2 m. Area 4,335 acres. The surface is rolling and hilly, and sends up a summit to the alt. of 745 ft. Only about 650 acres are in tillage and pasture. Kelp is manufactured along the shore, and iron-stone is found on the island.

ARRANMORE (South), the largest of the south isles of Arran. It is 61 m. in length, by 11 m. in breadth; and contains an area of 7,635 acres. Houses 451. Pop. 2,592, nearly all Catholics. The S side of the island is precipitous and bold. The highest ground rises to about 400 ft. above sea-level. The bay N from Killany and partly covered by Straw island, is the principal harbour in the Arran group. There is a lighthouse in the centre of the island.
ARRANFOWDDWY, a mountain of Wales, in Merionethshire, rising above Dolgelly to an alt. of 2,955 ft. above sea-level.
ARRANTANGY, a town of Hindostan, 50 m. SE from Trichinopoly, in N lat. 10° 10'. ARRARAPIRA. a town of Brazil, in the prov. of San Paulo, 25 m. NE of Bahia de Paranaguay.

ARRAS, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais.—The arrond. has an area of 135,677 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 163,032; in 1846, 167,598. It comprises the 9 cantons of Arras, Bapaume, Beautmetz-les-Loges, Bertincourt, Croisilles, Marquion, Pas, Viney, and Vitry. -The cant. which comprises 20 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 36,644.—The commune and town are on the r. or S bank of the Scarpe, at the embouchure of the Crinchon, in N lat. 50° 17′ 31″, E long. 2° 46′ 26″, 108 m. N by E of Paris by the road through Senlis and Peronne, and 215 kilom., or 135 m. by railway. The hotel de ville is a magnificent edifice; and the modern cathedral is in the pure Italian style. The public library contains 34,000 vols. Linen, tapestry, thread, and various cotton manufactures, form the chief productions of industry; there are also oil-mills, beet-root sugar refineries, salt-works, and brass-foundries. A., formerly the cap. of the Pays d'Artois, and now that of the dep., is a fortress of the 3d class. It is divided into two parts by a strong wall and deep ditch. The fortifications were constructed in 1670 by Vanban, the celebrated engineer. They consist of an irregular wall, guarded by ten bastions, with ravelins, lunettes, and a citadel, accounted the strongest in the kingdom.-This town is very ancient, and is supposed to be the Nemetacum which Cæsar took 50 B. C. It was pillaged by the Normans in 880, and, abandoned by its inhabitants, remained desolate for thirty years. In 901, it was taken by Charles the Simple. In 1492, the archduke Maximilian took it by treachery; in 1578, the prince of Orange captured it; in 1640, the French took it; and in 1654, the Spaniards attempted to retake it; but it was rescued by Marshal Turenne. It was the birth-place of Damiens, of Robespièrre, and of Joseph Lebon.
ARRAWUD, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Khandeish, 9 m. from Chapra, in N lat. 21° 10'. Pop. 3,000.
ARRAYAL DE ASSUMPCION, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyas, on the Toccantin, 340 m. NE of Villa Boa.
ARRAYAL DE PORATE, a town of Para, in Brazil, on the Toccantin, 210 m. SE of Para.
ARRAYAL DE RIO DAS ALMAS, a town of Brazil, prov. of Goyas, on the Toccantin, 126 m. NE of Villa Boa.
ARRAYOLLOS, a town of Portugal, prov. of Alemtejo, 12 m. N of Evora. Pop. 2,000.

ARREAU, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées. The cant. comprises 19 com. The town and com., 30 m. S of Tarbes, at the confluence of the Neste d'Aure, and Neste de Louron, have a pop. of 1,480.
ARRECIFE, a town of Buenos Ayres, on the high road from Lima to Buenos Ayres, on a river of the same name, in S lat. 34° 4'.

ARRE'E, a chain of mountains in France. They have different local names, and extend from E to W about 100 m., beginning in the dep. of Côtes-du- Nord, and terminating in that of Finistère. Their mean height is 900 ft. They are sterile, and broken with defiles.
ARRENIGMAWR, the highest summit of a group of mountains in the centre of Merionethshire. Alt. 2,809 ft. above sea-level.
ARRENS, a village of France, in the dep. of Hautes- Pyrénées, cant. of Aucun, 7 m. SW of Argèles.

ARRENTE (MONTE), a mountain of Tuscany, in the district of Siena, to the SW of Sovicille, celebrated for its fine marbles. ARRESITES, ARRESIFES, or ARRECISES, the most easterly of the Caroline islands, in the Pacific, in N lat. 10°, E long. 160° 24'.
ARRE'T, a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Valery-sur-Somme. Pop. 999.
ARRETON, a parish in the isle of Wight, 3 m. SE of Newport. Area 8,270 acres. Pop. 1,964.
ARREWAY, a town on the west coast of Celebes, in S lat. 0° 58', E long. 119° 15'.
ARRIEGE. See ARIEGE.
ARRIGORIAGO, a village of Spain in Biscay, on the l. bank of the Ybaizabel, 8 m. from Bilbao.
ARRINGTON, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 5 m. SE of Caxton. Area 1,388 acres. Pop. 317.
ARRION, a town of Azerdbijan, in Persia, 90 m. SE of Tabriz.
ARRISANA, a small island near the coast of Algarve in Portugal, in N lat. 37° 14′, W long. 8° 55′, lying opposite to a town of the same name on the main.
ARRIVACURCI, a small town of Coimbatore, in Hindostan, formerly belonging to Mysore, but ceded to Britain in 1799. It is 20 m. E of Daraporum.
ARRO (CANAL OF), an inlet in the gulf of Georgia, on the W coast of North America, to the E of Vancouver's island, in N lat. 48° 28', W long. 122° 19'.
ARROA (Long), an island in the Indian ocean, | Malalareen or Hart river, in the Koranas region in S ARR between Malaya and Sumatra, in N lat. 3° 0', E long. 100° 45'.

ARROCHAR, or Arroquhar, a parish in Dumbartonshire, on the banks of Loch Long. It is a mountainous district, and was at one time the land of the sept Macfarlane. Pop. in 1821, 376; in 1831, 559; in 1841, 580. Houses 103.

ARROE, an island of Denmark, lying in the S entrance of the Little Belt, between Alsen on the W, and Langeland on the E. It is about 14 m. in length from NW to SE, and has an area of 32 sq. m. Its surface is level, and the soil is rich and productive. Pop. 7,500. Its cap., Arroeskioebing, is on the E side; Marstall on the W side is nearly as large.

ARROE ISLANDS, or HARNISH ISLANDS, a cluster of islands in the entrance of the Red sea. The SW end of the Great Harnish is in N lat. 13° 39', E long. 42° 44'. It extends about 10 m. NE 1 N, and is 3 m. in breadth at its broadest. The W side is steep, with no soundings at 100 fath. close to. The Little Harnish is about 4 m. N of Great Harnish. The Sule Harnish islands, three in number, are about 2½ m. from the SE point of Great Harnish. They are chiefly inhabited by fishermen.

ARROESKIOEBING, the chief town in the island of Arröe, in the Little Belt, with a good harbour, protected by the opposite island of Deyeroe, with which Arröe is connected by a bridge. It is wellbuilt and stands on the SE side of the island, in N lat. 54° 53′, E long. 10° 35′. Pop. 1,400.
ARROH, a river of France, which runs into the Loire near Decize, in the dep. of Nièvre.
ARROIO DA CHINA, a town of La Plata, 192 m. NE of Buenos Ayres, on the Uruguay, 90 m. from its mouth.
ARRON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loire, cant. of Cloyes. Pop. 3,084.

ARRONCHES, a fortified town of Alemtejo, in Portugal, in the district of Portalegre, near the Spanish frontier, 95 m. E of Lisbon. Pop. 2,000.—Also a town of Brazil, 6 m. S of Seara. Cotton, rice, and cassada are cultivated in the neighbourhood.
ARRONES, a town of Navarre, in Spain, 7 m. S of Estella.
ARRONNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Mayet-de-Montagne. Pop. 1,060.

ARROS, a river of France, in the dep. of Landes, which joins the Adour on the r., near Izoges, after a course from SE to NW of 51 m., in which it passes Tournay and Plaisance.—Also a com. in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Nay. Pop. 1,100.
ARROU. See ARU.

ARROUX, a river of France, which rises near Culetre, and passes Arnay-le-Duc, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, and is navigable 9 m. before it falls into the Loire, near Digoin, after a course of 60 m. It receives the Creusevaux, Mesvrin, and Bourbince. It is the Arrosius of the middle ages.

ARROW, a river of Worcestershire, which joins the Avon near Bilford-Grange. -Also a river of Radnorshire, which joins the Lug near Leominster.—Also a river of Ireland, which issues from Lough-Arrow, and flows NNW to its confluence with the Owenbeg. -Also a lake in co. Sligo. It is 5 m. long, and covers 5,100 acres.—Also a village in Cheshire, in Woodchurch parish, pop. 122.—Also a parish in Warwickshire, 1 m. SW of Alcester. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. in 1841, 543.
ARROWSMITH'S ISLAND, one of the Mulgraves, in the Pacific ocean, in N lat. 6° 44', E long. 172° 20′.
ARROWSMITH MOUNT, a mountain in Beaufort co., W Australia, in Slat. 32° 10', Elong. 118° 7'.
ARROWSMITH RIVER, a head-branch of the Africa. The Mobatee river coming from the country of the Tamachas joins it in about lat. 27° 6' S .- Campbell.
ARROYA DE ELMEDINA, a village of Buenos Ayres, 165 m. NW of Buenos Ayres.
ARROYA DE LA CHINA, a town of Buenos Ayres, on the Uruguay, in S lat. 32° 29'.
ARROYA DE NANA, a river of Mexico, in the prov. of San Luis Potosi, falling into the Sabine river on the N shore of the gulf of Mexico.

ARROYO DE MOLINOS, a village of Spain, in Estremadura, between Alcuescar and Montanches, at the base of a range of hills which extend to the N and E in the form of a crescent. Lord Hill defeated General Girard at this village on the 28th of October, 1811.

ARROYO DEL PUERCO, a pueblo and village of Spain, in the prov. of Estremadura, 11 m. SW of Caceres. "In the parish-church of this miserable village are sixteen of the finest pictures ever painted by Morales." [Ford.] Pop. 5,060.
ARROYO SARCO, a large hacienda or village of Mexico, on the road between Tula and San Juan del Rio. The country around is wild, rocky, and uncultivated.
ARROYOS, a town of Buenos Ayres, 51 m. E of Assumpçion, in S lat. 25° 29' 36".
ARRUBAL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 8 m. E by S of Logrono, near the right bank of the Ebro.
ARS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of St.-Sulpice-des-Champs, 6 m. NW of Aubusson. Pop. 1,250.
ARS-SUR-MOSELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Gorze. Pop. 1,398. Good red wines are made here.

ARS-EN-RE', a commune and town of France, in the island of Rhé, about 20 m. W of La Rochelle, in N lat. 46° 12'. Pop. 3,875. Here are salt-works, and a small roadsted where large quantities of salt are shipped.

ARSA, a small river of Illyria, in the prov. of Istria. It rises near Safneviza, passes through the lake of Zepich, and flows into the gulf of Quarnero. It is navigable throughout the greater part of its course, which is about 21 m.
ARSAC, a commune and town of France, dep. of Gironde, cant. of Castelnau-de-Medoc. Pop. 640. Haut-Medoc wine is made here.
ARSACIDES. See SOLOMON'S ISLANDS.
ARSAGO, a town of Italy, 12 m. N of Milan.

ARSAMAS, a town and district of Russia, 48 m. S of Nishni-Novgorod, on the river Terchu. The inhabitants manufacture linen, leather, soap, dye-stuffs, potash, and other articles. The pop. is estimated at 7,000; that of the district at 120,000.
ARSEMINI, a town of Sardinia, 7 m. SW of Cagliari.
ARSEN, a river of Turkish Armenia, which falls into the Euphrates near Ilija, in N lat. 38° 25'.
ARSERINA, a small island of Africa, near the mouth of the Rio San Juan, in N lat. 19° 30', W long. 16°.
ARSIERO, a town of Italy, in Lombardy, 20 m. N of Vicenza. A very beautiful kind of marble is obtained here. Pop. 2,800.
ARSILLE. See ARZILLA.
ARSK, a town of Russia, in the district of Kasan, on the river Kasanka, 40 m. NE of Kasan. It contains about 150 houses, built of wood; and is inhabited by disbanded veterans.
ARSO, a river of Italy, in the prov. of Calabria Citra, flowing into the gulf of Taranto, in N lat. 39° 30'.
ARSOFFA, a town of Syria, 70 m. SE of Aleppo, in N lat. 35° 38′, E long. 38° 50′. ART
ARSOLI, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 13 m. NE of Tivoli. Pop. 2,200.
ARSTJO, a town of Russia, in Finland, on the shores of a large lake, 30 m. NE of Borga.
ARSU, a town of Persia, in the province of Shirvan, on the Kur, 40 m. SW of Shamaghi.
ARSUNDA, a town of Sweden, in the prov. of Gestrikland, 15 m. SW of Gefle.
ARSU'F, a ruined village of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, 9 m. N of Jaffa, at the mouth of the Nahr-el-Arsuf. It is supposed to mark the site of the ancient Apollonia.
ARSURA, a small island, 30 m. N of Pelagnisi, in the Grecian archipelago, in N lat. 39° 30'.
ARSURES (LES), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Arbois. Pop. 272.
ARSUS, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Aleppo, on the gulf of Iskenderun, 10 m. NE of Ras-el- Khanzir. It is perhaps the Rhesus of Strabo.

ARTA, or Larta, a town of Lower Albania—the ancient Ambracia probably—on the E or l. bank of the Arta, 70 m. NW of Lepanto, 20 m. NE of Prevesa, and 7 from the shores of the gulf to which it gives its name, in N lat. 39° 8', E long. 20° 59'. Pop. 7,000. The river-bed is here about 200 yards across; but is filled with sand-banks in the dry season. The ancient circumf. of the walls embraces four times the extent of the present town. [Urquhart.] A. is a Greek metropolitan see, and the residence of several European consuls. It carries on a considerable trade; and is the entrepot between Yanina and the gulf. Its port is Salahora; and it exports cattle, wine, tobacco, cotton, articles of dress, capotes, leather, hemp, flax, &c. It suffered dreadfully during the Greek revolutionary struggle.—The gulf of Arta, the ancient Ambracius sinus, is an inlet of the Ionian sea, between the parallels of 38° 52', and 39° 3' N, bounded by the Turkish prov. of Epirus and the Greek prov. of Acarnania. Its extreme length from E to W is about 25 m.; its breadth 10 m. The entrance is narrow. Between a bastion of the walls of Prevesa on the N, and Fort La Punta on the S, it is only 700 yards across. Its greatest depth is 36 fath. The N shore has an elevation of about 60 ft.; towards the S the coasts are flat and swampy. The principal streams falling into it are the Luro and the Arta.—The latter river rises on the flanks of Pindus. Its width at its mouth is about 60 yards in the dry season, but above it soon becomes much more narrow. Long flats of stiff black mud and weeds line the shores of the gulf on both sides of the mouth of this river, which meanders in a tortuous course, and with a slow current, through the rich plains of Arta. It is generally identified with the ancient Aracthus. The gulf abounds in mullet, soles, eels, prawns, and sardines. It has a rise and fall of tide of about 2 ft.
